summary: My family was living in Oakland, California when the war started. We were taken from there to the Tanforan Assembly Center shortly after the attack on Pearl Harbor. Oakland was where my parents operated a flower nursery before we lost our livelihood due to the war. It was the place we called home before everything changed.

After leaving Oakland, my family was taken to the Tanforan Assembly Center. We stayed there for about nine months before being moved to Topaz War Relocation Center in Utah. Tanforan was one of the first places where Japanese Americans were held after the internment orders. It was a difficult time, but the camp had schools and activities for young people.

We were moved from Tanforan to the Topaz War Relocation Center in Utah, where we stayed for the duration of the war. I was just four years old when we arrived and we were among the last to leave the camp. My mother worked cleaning the laundry room and latrine at night, while my father had an administrative job. Life in Topaz was a mix of hardship for the adults but surprisingly good times for the kids with schools and sumo tournaments.

After leaving the camps, my family returned to San Francisco and was placed in barracks in Hunter's Point. We rented a flat near Japantown on Pine Street, where I eventually got married. San Francisco was where we tried to rebuild our lives, but it was hard facing discrimination and taunts at school. Despite the challenges, it became home again after the war.

When we came back from the camps, my family was put in barracks down in Hunter's Point, San Francisco. Some of those buildings are still there today. It was a tough transition from camp life to living in the city, especially dealing with the hostility from other kids at school. Hunter's Point was the first place we stayed as we tried to find our footing again.

I remember the day of the attack on Pearl Harbor vividly. My sister and I were walking that Sunday, and the sky was full of black smoke. We could feel the earth shake and saw the attack from a distance. At the time, we didn’t fully understand what was happening, but my mother looked worried and my father soon became an air raid warden.

The attack on Pearl Harbor was a shocking event that changed everything for us. It happened on a Sunday morning in 1941, and I remember seeing the sky filled with smoke and feeling the earth shake. That event led to my father becoming an air raid warden and eventually to the internment of Japanese Americans like my family. It was the beginning of a very difficult chapter in our lives.

After the camps, my mother found a place for us to live in the Haight-Ashbury neighborhood of San Francisco. A friend of hers owned the flat and we rented the bottom unit. It was one of the first places we stayed as we tried to rebuild our lives after the war. This neighborhood was part of our early post-internment experience.

Eventually, we moved to a flat right outside of Japantown on Pine Street in San Francisco. Japantown was a central place for the Japanese American community and where I lived when I got married. It was a place where many young people from the camps connected and rebuilt their social lives. Japantown became a symbol of resilience for us after the war.

My family lived on Pine Street in San Francisco, right outside of Japantown, after we left the camps. It was there that I got married and where our community gathered. Pine Street was home to many Japanese Americans who were rebuilding their lives and reconnecting after the internment. It was also near the Pine United Methodist Church where I met my husband.

My family attended Pine United Methodist Church in San Francisco, which played an important role in my life. It was through this church and the influence of Reverend Lloyd Wake that I met my husband. The church became a gathering place for many young Japanese Americans coming out of the camps, serving as both a spiritual and social center.

Reverend Lloyd Wake was a key figure in my life and in the Japanese American community in San Francisco. Through his influence at Pine United Methodist Church, I met my husband. He helped create a welcoming space for young people coming out of the camps to connect and rebuild their lives. Reverend Wake’s leadership was very important during that time.

Before coming to San Francisco, my husband Art had a brief stint at Pacific Union College in Angwin, California. After that, he was 'adopted' by a Caucasian family who were active at the Temple Methodist Church. His time at Pacific Union College was part of his early life before he became involved with the Japanese American community in San Francisco.

Art’s adoptive family was very active at the Temple Methodist Church, which was mostly white. They encouraged him to attend a Japanese church to meet Japanese girls, which led him to Pine United Methodist Church where he met me. The Temple Methodist Church was part of his early social and religious life after leaving Hawaii and before settling in San Francisco.

The Japanese American internment during World War II was a devastating experience for my family and community. We were forcibly removed from our home in Oakland and sent first to Tanforan and then to Topaz. My parents lost their livelihood and dignity, and the disruption to family life was profound. Though the camps tried to provide activities for young people, the emotional and social scars lasted a lifetime.

The Japanese language was an important part of our culture, but after the internment, many of us struggled with our identity. The camps and later life in America challenged us to balance our heritage with assimilation. Though I didn’t speak much about it at the time, the language connected us to our roots and community, especially in places like Japantown and the Japanese churches.

I see parallels between the treatment of Japanese Americans during World War II and how Muslims and Muslim Americans are treated today. The internment was driven by irrational fear and racism, especially on the West Coast, and now the whole country is gripped by fear against Muslims. It’s heartening to see many Americans standing up against this injustice, but I’m deeply concerned about the leadership that fuels such fear.
